blocks.content:
System Design Interview Patterns Cheat Sheet
Distilled from 32 real system design problems: Uber, YouTube, WhatsApp, Ticketmaster, Dropbox, and more.
1. The 4 System Archetypes
Every system design problem maps to one of four archetypes. Identify it first — it dictates your entire tech stack.

rows: 100% accurate data served fast to millions
Ticketmaster, URL Shortener, Yelp, Delivery System
No race conditions on critical writes
Uber, Online Auction, Tinder, Lyft Matching
Ingest millions of events/sec, never block
Ad Clicks, WhatsApp, FB Live Comments, LeetCode
FB News Feed, YouTube, Google News, Instagram

blocks.content:
2. File / Media Upload Pattern (S3 Multipart)
Used in: YouTube, Dropbox, Instagram, WhatsApp, Yelp, Netflix
Flow
• Client requests upload → Service calls S3 → gets uploadId + presigned URLs per chunk
• Client uploads chunks in parallel; tracks which chunks succeeded
• On connection drop: call S3 ListParts(uploadId) to find missing chunks, re-upload only those
• Client calls CompleteMultipartUpload → S3 assembles single object → fires s3:ObjectCreated event
• Upload service receives event → saves final S3 URL to DB
Post-Upload Processing Pipeline
• Quarantine bucket first — virus scan + content validation before moving to public bucket
• Rechunker worker (async) — re-chunks file per system needs (e.g., video frames)
• Transcoder worker (async) — produces 240p/480p/720p/1080p/4K variants
• Manifest file — references all chunks; uploaded to CDN for popular content
• Client-side adaptive bitrate — fetches appropriate chunk per network/device from manifest
Key Rules
• Always include file size limits in presigned URL conditions — prevents storage bombs
• Store uploadId in localStorage so uploads survive app restarts
• Set S3 lifecycle policy to clean incomplete uploads after 1–2 days (they cost money)
• Use range requests (HTTP Range header) for resumable downloads too

3. Real-Time Delivery Pattern
Used in: WhatsApp, FB Live Comments, Lyft tracking, LeetCode results, Ticketmaster, Strava

rows: Live scores, stock tickers, AI token streaming, feed updates
Chat, collaborative editing, live tracking, multiplayer

blocks.content:
Scaling WebSocket Servers
• Use consistent hashing to route users to the same server instance
• Store server→user mapping in Zookeeper or Redis so any instance can route
• On server failure: new instance takes over; clients reconnect; Zookeeper updates mapping
• For offline delivery: store messages in per-user Redis queue (TTL=30 days); flush on reconnect
4. Geospatial / Location Pattern
Used in: Uber, Lyft (all 4 services), Yelp, Delivery System, Tinder
Storage Options

rows: No persistence; shard by geohash prefix for scale
Business search, polygon queries, source of truth
Complex query planning; PostgreSQL scale limits
Sync lag from PostgreSQL via Kafka
Surge zones, sharding key, cache key for ETAs
Cross-cell boundary queries need neighbor lookup
Uniform area cells, better proximity math

blocks.content:
Geohash Sharding Pattern
• Shard Redis by geohash prefix (4 chars ≈ 40km cells)
• Proximity query fans out to target cell + 8 neighbors → parallelized, <10ms
• Driver offline detection: no heartbeat for 30s → remove from geospatial index
• Apply Kalman filter to smooth GPS noise before storing
5. Caching Patterns

rows: Write to DB and cache atomically
Read from cache; miss → load from DB → populate
High consistency needed — race window on miss
Write to cache first; async flush to DB
Data loss on cache crash unacceptable
Cache sits in front; auto-loads on miss
Push data into cache on write (news feed)

blocks.content:
Cache Invalidation
• Version-based: push new version to cache; old version phases out via TTL/LRU
• Delete-on-write: delete key on DB update; next request repopulates (cache-aside)
• CDC (Change Data Capture): Debezium streams DB changes → Kafka → invalidate cache key
• Short TTL as safety net: even if invalidation fails, stale data expires
Hot Key Problem
• Spread hot key across N sub-keys (post123-0..post123-N); readers sum all sub-keys
• Probabilistic early refresh: refresh at 50min if TTL is 60min — prevents thundering herd
• Request coalescing: combine concurrent misses for same key into one backend call
6. Database Selection Guide

rows: Sub-millisecond reads / counters at huge QPS
Known access patterns, massive scale, simple key lookups
Heavy text search with relevance, prefixes, facets

blocks.content:
Rule of thumb: Default to Postgres. Add Redis for speed. Add Elasticsearch for search. Choose DynamoDB when you have a stable partition key and need near-limitless scale.
7. Queue / Messaging Selection Guide

rows: Massive throughput, ordered by key, replay history
SNS + SQS per consumer, or Kafka topics
Fast lightweight work queues, modest durability
Workflow engine (Step Functions, Temporal) + outbox pattern

blocks.content:
Rule of thumb: Default to SQS for jobs. Kafka for high-scale streams and replay. Redis Streams for fast lightweight queues. Pub/Sub only when loss is acceptable.
8. Concurrency & Contention Patterns

rows: Lock row upfront; blocks others until commit
Same-row hot writes (stock inventory, seat booking)
Version number; retry only on conflict
Profile updates, wiki pages, low-traffic CMS
Coordinator: Prepare → Commit across all DBs
Sequence of local txns; compensating txns on failure
Redis SETNX/Redlock or Zookeeper; one process at a time
Prevent double-claim on drivers, limited offers
Soft lock (TTL) holds resource for X minutes

blocks.content:
Deadlock Prevention
• Always acquire locks in a consistent deterministic order (e.g., sort by user_id before locking)
• Set transaction timeouts as a fallback; most DBs have automatic deadlock detection
9. Pagination

rows: Risk of duplicates/skips if rows change
O(N) scan — degrades at high offsets
Small static datasets; 'Jump to page X'

blocks.content:
Rule: Always use cursor-based pagination for feeds and large datasets. Use a stable unique sort key (created_at + id). Pass an opaque cursor to the client.
10. Search Patterns
Full-Text Search Stack
• Source of truth: PostgreSQL stores canonical data
• Search index: Elasticsearch synced via Kafka CDC (Debezium)
• Ranking: BM25 relevance + distance + rating + recency (weights tuned via A/B)
• Semantic search: Pre-compute embeddings → store in Faiss vector DB → merge with BM25 results
• Autocomplete: Elasticsearch prefix queries or Redis sorted sets (ZADD with score=0, lex order)
Sync Pattern: PostgreSQL → Elasticsearch
• Debezium captures DB changes → publishes to Kafka → ES consumer updates index in <1s
• On data drift: re-index from DB snapshot; use index aliases to swap without downtime
11. Rate Limiting Algorithms

rows: Bucket fills at fixed rate; each request consumes a token
Allows bursts up to bucket size
Requests queue; processed at fixed rate
Count requests per time window (e.g., per minute)
Boundary burst: 2x rate at window edge
Log each request timestamp; count in rolling window
Weighted blend of current + previous window counts

blocks.content:
• Store counters in Redis with TTL. Use Lua scripts for atomic check-and-increment.
• Distribute rate limiting across nodes using consistent hashing on user_id.
• Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.
12. Fan-Out Patterns (News Feed / Notifications)
Fan-out on Write (Push)
• On post creation: write post to each follower's feed cache immediately
• Pro: O(1) read — feed is pre-built in Redis list
• Con: Celebrity with 100M followers = 100M writes per post (fan-out storm)
• Fix: Hybrid — push to regular users; celebrities use pull at read time
Fan-out on Read (Pull)
• At read time: fetch posts from all followed users and merge
• Pro: No write amplification
• Con: O(N) read — slow for users following thousands of accounts
Hybrid (Recommended for large systems)
• Push to followers with <1000 followers; pull from celebrities (>1M followers) at read time
• Use Bloom filters to skip already-seen content
• Use Kafka to trigger async fan-out workers; batch followers in groups of 100
13. Consistency Patterns
Read-Your-Writes
• After a write, pin the user's session to the primary DB for 10 seconds
• Or: route reads to primary if request timestamp < last_write_timestamp + 10s
Eventual Consistency Signals
• View counts, like counts, follower counts — eventual consistency is fine
• Use Redis INCR for counters; flush to DB every 60s
• For viral content: shard counter across N Redis keys; sum at read time
Outbox Pattern (Guaranteed Event Delivery)
• Write business data AND event to same DB transaction (outbox table)
• Separate poller/CDC reads outbox → publishes to Kafka → marks as sent
• Guarantees event is only published if DB write succeeded
14. Distributed Systems Fundamentals
Consistent Hashing
• Hash nodes and keys onto a ring; walk clockwise to find node
• Adding/removing a node only moves ~1/N of keys (vs. full reshuffle with modulo)
• Virtual nodes: each server maps to multiple ring points → even load distribution
• Use for: Redis cluster sharding, CDN routing, session affinity
CAP Theorem in Practice

rows: Slightly stale position is fine; must stay online
Double-booking is catastrophic; prefer to fail
Deliver out-of-order; sequence numbers restore order
Money must be consistent; availability is secondary

blocks.content:
Idempotency
• Assign a UUID idempotency key per operation (user_id + action + timestamp)
• Before processing: check Redis/DB for existing key → return cached result if found
• Prevents double-charge, double-bid, duplicate job execution

15. Async Job Queue Patterns
Reliability Patterns
• Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): After 3–5 failures, move job to DLQ for human inspection
• Heartbeat: Worker pings queue every N seconds; if missed, job is re-queued
• Idempotency keys: Deduplicate jobs before processing; return existing result
• Backpressure: Return 503 when queue depth exceeds threshold; autoscale workers on queue depth (not CPU)
Job Dependency Orchestration
• Simple chains: each worker enqueues the next job before marking itself complete
• Complex workflows: use AWS Step Functions, Temporal, or Airflow
• Mixed workloads: separate queues by expected duration (fast/slow) to prevent head-of-line blocking
16. Monitoring & Observability
Metrics Pipeline
• Agents on each host collect metrics → push to Kafka → stream processor (Flink) aggregates
• Time-series DB (Prometheus/TimescaleDB) stores aggregated metrics
• Alerting rules trigger on threshold breaches; PagerDuty/OpsGenie for on-call
Key Metrics to Track
• Queue depth (not CPU) for autoscaling workers
• ETA accuracy (predicted vs. actual) for routing services
• Cache hit rate — if low, reconsider caching strategy
• P99 latency — not just average; tail latency matters
• Replication lag — for read replicas and CDC pipelines
17. Security & Privacy Patterns
• Presigned URLs: Generate time-limited S3 URLs server-side; client uploads/downloads directly
• End-to-end encryption: Signal Protocol (X3DH + Double Ratchet); server never sees plaintext
• Crypto-shredding (GDPR): Encrypt user data with per-user key; delete key to render data unreadable
• Soft deletes: Set deleted_at timestamp; nullify PII but keep ID for financial records
• Sandbox isolation: gVisor/Firecracker for untrusted code; seccomp-bpf syscall filters
• Abuse prevention: Quarantine bucket → scan → approve before making content public
18. Quick Reference: Problem → Pattern

rows: S3 Multipart Upload + presigned URLs + resumable chunks
SSE or WebSocket + Kafka fan-out + Redis pre-computed list
Redis distributed lock (SETNX) + pessimistic DB row lock
Read replicas → Redis cache → CDN (in that order)
Shard DB → Kafka queue → batch writes → NoSQL LSM-Tree
OS file watcher → sync agent → SSE notification → pull delta
Auto-increment ID → Base62 encode (or SHA-256 → take N bits)
Redis INCR (sharded for viral) → flush to DB every 60s
Per-user Redis queue (TTL=30d) → flush on reconnect
Split into 2-min segments → parallel GPU workers → HLS manifest
Sliding window counters in Redis → ML model every 60s → push via WebSocket
Two-level URL frontier + Bloom filter dedup + consistent hash by domain
Kafka + Redis sorted set (score=run_time) + worker pool
Token bucket in Redis + Lua atomic script + 429 response
